topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, stark white against the worn wood of my desk, felt like a physical slap. I reread the paragraph three times, my jaw tightening until my molars ached. This entire institution, the place I’d poured years of late nights and desperate ambition into, was just… dissolving. I slammed the paper onto the filing cabinet, the sound echoing too loudly in the suddenly cavernous office. "Unbelievable," I muttered, the word tasting like ash. My hands were shaking, a frantic, restless energy buzzing just beneath my skin. I paced the small space, kicking at the loose floorboard near the window, needing to expend some of the churning pressure building in my chest. This wasn't just a setback; it was a total erasure of everything I thought mattered.

[story 2]

I stared at the closing announcement on my phone screen, the fluorescent light of the coffee shop reflecting harshly off my face. A slow, cold burn started low in my stomach and spread outward, making my knuckles white where I gripped the ceramic mug. I didn't bother to look at the barista when she handed me the lukewarm latte. "So, that's it?" I asked, my voice dangerously level, devoid of any warmth. I took a sharp, ragged breath, the air catching painfully in my throat. I wanted to shout, to tear the laminated notice into confetti, but I kept my mouth shut, forcing the raw surge of frustration down into a tight, controlled knot in my chest. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping back against the tile floor, and walked out into the indifferent afternoon, every step heavy with suppressed fury.

[story 3]

The administrative meeting was a suffocating exercise in forced calm. Everyone spoke in hushed, rehearsed tones about "realignments" and "future prospects," but all I could focus on was the way the chair in front of me felt too small, the way the polished mahogany table seemed designed to mock my desperation. When the final word was spoken—the definitive end—a deep, visceral reaction took over. My vision narrowed, and I could feel a heat radiating off my skin, prickling the hairs on my arms. I leaned forward, resting my forearms on the table, my gaze fixed on the speaker with an intensity that bordered on hostility. "You think this is over?" I managed, the sound brittle and sharp. I pushed back from the table, the sudden movement jarring, and walked out as if propelled by a physical force, needing to escape the confines of that sterile room.
